01 / 13
Ivana Tso · Case Study
Conveyor×Trust Center·2026

Now & Next.

A Trust Center Built by Listening.

What we shipped, what we sprinted toward, and what nine customers told us along the way.

Read the backstory

What’s a Trust Center? A public-facing portal where B2B software companies publish the security artifacts (SOC 2, ISO certifications, privacy policies) that buyers need to vet them as a vendor. A great one closes the deal in minutes; a bad one delays procurement for weeks.

Track one - the midway ship. A focused above-the-fold feature update that would alleviate friction our customers were feeling now. Practical, evidence-backed, in-market in weeks - not quarters. The bet that earned us trust to keep going.

Track two - the squad’s first vision sprint. Three days where we imagined what a newly designed Trust Center could feel like in 2027 - agentic, AI-native, touchless. The bet that pointed at where we wanted to be by year-end.

Designer
Ivana Tso
Sr. Product Designer
Squad
Three people

Q1 2026
Six weeks

02 / 13
The Brief
The Brief

"Make the Trust Center better."

Every B2B security review starts at the Trust Center, and visitors take 30 seconds to decide if they need to file a ticket for more help. Before making improvements, we needed to establish a baseline.

Pre-authentication
Entering the Trust Center
What a user sees publicly before receiving access approval. Note: This project was post launch of the Trust Center Agent, and there were Trust Centers without the top header as well.
3.3/5
Authenticated
Approved access to documents
Once approved, users could see a Just for You section, custom signed documents, and have access to the Trust Center Agent, a new feature launched (see separate case study) for authenticated users.
3.1/5
Pre-authentication
"I feel confident I can find what I need"
13%1
13%2
25%3
38%4
13%5
avg 3.3 · n=8
Authenticated
Same prompt, post-auth
14%1
14%2
14%3
57%4
0%5
avg 3.1 · n=7
Strongly disagree Neutral Strongly agree

Our stakeholders had opinions and experience, but we wanted customer voices to shape the work. The two baselines came from an async survey, and many respondents stayed on to share their frustrations with us. We listened across two personas - the Admins running Trust Centers, and the TPRMs visiting them - and designed for both.

What the team thought, before customers spoke
Before talking to a single customer, the squad ran an internal benchmarking workshop. We mapped our user persona, the jobs to be done, our current state, and where we wanted the Trust Center to go.
Continue
03 / 13
The Arc
The Arc

Where this case study fits.

The Trust Center has gone through various iterations. In this project, we focus on the third panel and how we look towards the fourth as a North Star.

Oct 2024 · Baseline
What a visitor saw first.

Once authenticated, the very first visual a visitor sees when entering a Trust Center didn't show any important information, leaving bad impressions before any scrolling or searching started.

Jan 2025 · Agent added
An agent appeared.

ConveyorAI joined the right rail. Visitors could now ask cited questions instead of scrolling. The first sign that the Trust Center could become conversational.

Read more about Trust Center Agent
March 2026 · The Now
A surface that listens back.

The midway ship. Search in the header, product line filtering, branded nav, pinned announcements - every change traceable to a customer who told us why.

April 2026 · The Next
Touchless review.

From the squad’s first vision sprint. The Trust Center as a working surface - upload a questionnaire, the agent completes it. The leap Sales asked us for. The vision of touchless review.

Why this matters

Our focus on the mid-way ship was to drive value as we make the vision of Touchless Review possible. This mid-way ship isn't a finished line, but a rung on the ladder that takes us from the baseline to a Trust Center re-imagined for our customers.

For both iterations, every choice we made, we asked these two questions: Does it deliver value our customers can feel today? Does it move us towards the agentic future our sales team needs to win against our competitors like SafeBase and Vanta?

Continue
04 / 13
02 · Orient

A/B Testing Prototypes.

Two prototype directions with 15 different variations that we could drill into for structured comparable data from our customers.

Open Layout
Inline quick links · Recently Updated
Live prototype ↗
Card (Closed) Layout
Sidebar quick links · Pinned Announcement
Live prototype ↗
2 days
Built in Lovable. I started with creating a clear Figma mockup and recreating the mockup in Lovable as a base. From there, I was able to build two variants to confirm direction with stakeholders, and from there move straight into customer testing.
15
Above-the-fold changes scoped across each user's viewport, from a table of contents to search bar types, language changes, onboarding modals, and feedback modules.
9
Participants across Admin, Visitor, dual, and internal-stakeholder lenses.
A constraint that shaped every decision

We didn’t have a staging environment for individual Trust Centers. Customers couldn’t preview changes on their own Trust Center before they went live - which meant every decision had to be safe enough to ship to all customers at once, including enterprise accounts.

That constraint shaped how we tested and what we were willing to bet on. We couldn’t ship and iterate in the wild. We had to listen, decide, and commit - all before pushing a single pixel to production.

Continue

07 / 13
Findings · Decisions

Four findings. One bet.

When we synthesized 135 ratings across nine voices, four patterns emerged. Three pointed at what to ship - one pointed at what to let go.

01
The Layout Finding

No one preferred all of one layout.

Across nine external participants, every single one cherry-picked across the two prototypes. The shipping spec became a hybrid - each pick traceable to a specific majority.

See the per-feature matrix
ComponentWinning versionTally
Updated Header / Top BarCard · "All Products" + "Resources"5 / 7 - strongest single-version win
Right-side content areaCard · Pinned Announcement4 / 7
Search bar + Product LineOpen · Integrated filter4 / 7
Onboarding ModalCard · Two-step w/ product lineSlight edge - admin-favored
Your Documents locationOpen · User profile popup3 / 7
Table of ContentsCard · Horizontal section bar (slight)3 Card · 2 Open · 2 neutral
Banner / Logo treatmentNo overlap (slight)2 · 2 · 3 neutral
02
The AI Finding

Same product. Opposite reactions.

When senior admins saw ConveyorAI, they braced. When visitors saw it, they exhaled. Same surface, opposite reactions - the design pattern wasn’t whether the AI was good. It was making prominence a dial, not a default.

Admin · Cvent

“Enterprise customers are very leery when they see the word AI. As soon as they see AI, they’re going to go: well, what are you doing with my data?”

J.N. · Lead Analyst, Cvent
Visitor · TPRM

“Where were you 10 years ago?”

B.C. · Director, Security GRC & AI Governance
03
The Timeline Finding

Two truths, both right.

Visitors wanted us to ship the small fixes now. Sales wanted us to swing for an AI-native rebuild. We chose to hold both - shipping the midway feature this quarter, sprinting the vision next. Neither voice got everything. Both got moved closer.

Admin

“Do it in a week, if you can, seriously. I would welcome that.”

J.N. · Lead Analyst, Cvent
Sales · Competitive lens

“This is a step in the right direction. It does feel like a step versus tearing this thing down to the studs.”

D.B. · Head of Sales, Conveyor
How we held both voices in scope

We split the work into two parallel tracks. Track one: the immediate ship - friction removers our visitors had been asking for, sized to be testable in six weeks. Track two: the north-star vision sprint we'd run in Q2, where Sales got their bigger swing - an AI-native Trust Center concept that explicitly responds to what D.B. and our competitive deals were telling us.

Naming both tracks early let us make a smaller bet without making it look like we'd abandoned the bigger one. The interim ship became a proof point for the vision sprint, not a substitute.

04
The Deprioritization Finding

The feature I was sure about didn’t matter.

I was sure customers would lean on a Table of Contents - long pages, dense docs, a lot to scan. Then we tested. Nine walkthroughs, zero unprompted mentions. Two of our most engaged participants had already built their own pattern: Ctrl+F. The rest didn’t see it at all. The decision wasn’t to redesign it - it was to deprioritize.

Visitor · Notion

“Where is this table of contents? What table of contents, no?... I did not see that at all.”

R.T. · TPRM, Notion
Where the time went

Engineering capacity reinvested in an accessibility fix R.B. flagged on active-tab contrast - a separate issue surfaced by the same finding.

Synthesis · R.B., colorblind UX

The bet wasn’t one decision. It was four of them - three held in tension, one deliberately let go - all shaped by the customers who told us where the tension lived in the first place.

Continue
08 / 13
04 · Act
04 · Act

The bet, and how to ship it.

A defensible recommendation isn't "ship everything." I sorted the 15 changes into four tiers, then paired them with a rollout sequence customers told us they wanted.

Ship Global Search · Updated Header · Pinned Announcements · Your Documents 7/7 align
Configure Onboarding Modal · Product Line Selector · Table of Contents Mixed signal
Reconsider Changelog (admin opt-in only, audit timing risk surfaced) 1 scared rating
Future Mobile optimization · Layout style standardization Out of scope

The rollout customers told us they wanted

1

Advance Notice

1–2 weeks minimum, email notification

2

Preview Link

See it on their own TC before it goes live

3

Launch

Roll out to all TCs. No opt-in required.

4

Rollback

"Nice to have" safety net. Not expected to be used.

The synthesis we handed to engineering

Each tier above was backed by per-feature synthesis tiles - quick visual references showing which version won, who dissented, and why. Engineering didn't need to take notes; the decisions were already auditable in customer voice.

Search + Product Line Open · integrated → ship
Header / Top Bar Card · branded nav → ship
Right-side content Card · Pinned → ship
Your Documents Open · profile → ship
Onboarding Modal Configurable + dismissible
Changelog Reconsider · opt-in
Continue

10 / 13
Live · In market
Live in market

The bet shipped without surprise.

The midway ship rolled out behind a feature flag with a 2-week preview window. Every Trust Center editor saw an in-app tour announcing the change. The numbers below are real, captured in production.

173

active Trust Centers receiving the rollout. Powering trust at every customer named below.

0

emails back with concerns. Out of 3.2K Trust Center editors who saw the announcement - and zero concerns from the top strike-zone customers we checked in with directly before launch.

Announcement tour - real-world engagement
3.2K
Editors started the tour
296
Clicked "Remind Me"
9.3%
207
Previewed their Trust Center
6.5%
2.3K
Dismissed (acknowledged)
72%
503 editors (16%) engaged with preview or remind. The other 72% acknowledged and moved on. Zero objections in either group - and zero from the top strike-zone customers we proactively checked in with before launch.
Powering trust for customers including
Cvent Workday DocuSign Symplr Notion Atlassian Alteryx Arctic Wolf PRGX +164 more
Read more about the rollout mechanics

The rollout matched what customers asked for in research, step for step.

  • Advance notice. Every active Trust Center admin received an in-app tour 2 weeks before the visual change went live, linking to a preview URL on their own TC.
  • Preview window. Customers could click through to app.conveyor.com/trust-center/coming-soon/ to see exactly what their visitors would experience post-launch.
  • Remind Me. 151 customers asked to be reminded closer to launch day. Those reminders are scheduled and queued.
  • No opt-out. Customers told us they didn't want fragmented experiences across Trust Centers. The rollout went to all 173 simultaneously, with rollback as a safety net (not expected to be used, per research).
  • Feature flag. Engineering deployed behind a flag with a 3-month rollback window. Zero rollbacks requested to date.
Continue
11 / 13
What's Next · Vision Sprint
What's Next · Vision Sprint

Three days that pointed at 2027.

The interim move shipped. The next loop turned the north star into a focused Q2 bet - six people, six prototypes built in parallel, three days of structured convergence.

From ambiguous to aligned

100%

of external participants comfortable with a near-term rollout. Not a single objection raised about the rollout itself.

6 wks

from a vague brief to a tiered shipping recommendation, a rollout plan, and a research artifact teams kept revisiting.

  • 135 sentiment ratings turned into one defensible decision matrix.
  • Per-feature heatmap surfaced the one risky change (Changelog) before it shipped.
  • Sales lead and Marketing lead reached consensus despite different lenses.
  • Engineering received a tier-prioritized scope, not a wish list.
The vision statement

"Trust Center Visitors can confidently and efficiently complete their security review without support when using a Conveyor hosted Trust Center."

Day 1 · Mar 31
Diverge & Build
Heads-down. Each person builds independently. AI prototypes, sketches, code, whatever moves fastest. Showable, not perfect.
Day 2 · Apr 1
Show & Debate
10-minute show & tells. Find the convergence. Surface the tension. Sleep on it overnight.
Day 3 · Apr 2
Converge & Decide
Force-rank by impact x feasibility. Lock the bets. Walk away with a Q2 scope and a list of what we're not building.

My contribution: a comprehensive vision called Coco - three pillars: an AI agent, a Trust Scorecard, and comparison tools. Detailed on the next slide →

Where we converged

  • AI as interface, not feature. Not a chatbot bolted on - the way people interact with the Trust Center.
  • Constrained flexibility wins. Templates and pre-built blocks beat free-form editing every time.
  • Transparency is the differentiator. Genuinely useful security info is the real moat.

Q2 priorities locked

  • Trust Center Agent (reimagined). Primary differentiator. Unify with search.
  • Visual / design themes. Templates with constraints. Brand alignment without ugly outputs.
  • Staging environments. Foundational. Lightweight scope. Builds confidence for future rollouts.
Read more · Sprint structure, the six prototypes, and what we cut

The structure: Diverge - Show & Tell - Debate - Converge - Decide. Pre-work shared one week prior. Async absorption of 8 reference docs (competitor research, customer feedback themes, Above-the-Fold research, vision FigJam, Q2 goals).

What the team built (six theses, one north star):

• Ivana (me) - Coco AI agent + Trust Scorecard + comparison tools.
• Chris - Search-first TC with scroll-based storytelling. Crawl-walk-run.
• Sydney - Three prototypes: AI builder with templates, sales enablement, ROI dashboard.
• H.T. - Onboarding flow + designer canvas. AI scrapes the customer's site for branding.
• Anner - Hybrid builder combining chat agent with manual controls.
• Nadim - Embeddable Trust Center components customers place on their own websites.

What we explicitly cut from Q2 scope: Full AI-powered design agent (too complex). Heavy analytics dashboard UI (start with API/MCP foundation). Deep sales enablement features. My full Trust Scorecard / reputation system (Phase 2+). Traditional enterprise workflows.

The clarifying question: Who are we building for? The team aligned that the primary design target is strike-zone prospects not currently using Conveyor - upper mid-market and lower enterprise companies with capacity problems who want touchless automation.

Continue
12 / 13
The Vision Prototype
The Coco prototype

What I brought to the vision sprint.

Three pillars, one thesis: a Trust Center that genuinely helps third-party risk managers assess security - not one that performs compliance theater.

1 / 9
Trust Center vision
PILLAR 01
Coco, the AI agent

Conversational and voice-enabled. Handles search, questionnaires, and review checklists in one interface. Pick your Coco character on entry; it stays with you across the visit.

PILLAR 02
Trust Scorecard

Quantifies coverage, freshness, and framework alignment. Category scores and content gaps surface at a glance. Exportable for boardrooms and QBRs.

PILLAR 03
Comparison tools

Visitors stack a vendor against peers - response time, doc freshness, certification breadth - so “which vendor passes our bar?” becomes a one-screen answer.

Read more · the wider vision (dark mode, sessions, MCP, badges)

Beyond the three pillars, the prototype explored a wider set of moves the team could make in 2027:

• Dark mode for visitors who review at night or prefer the contrast.
• Session history - pick up a security review where you left off, even days later.
• MCP server integration - expose Trust Center content to other AI tools the visitor uses.
• Badge gamification for admins - visible signals when frameworks are clean and docs are fresh.

The Q2 squad scoped the full Scorecard / reputation system as Phase 2 work - aligned with a longer-arc “network thesis” for the platform. The Coco AI agent and the visual / design themes carried into Q2 priorities directly.

Live prototype

13 / 13
Reflection
Closing

What I'm taking forward.

Six lessons from the loop. The ones I'll carry into the next bet, and the one after that.

01

Quantitative meets qualitative.

Numbers alone don't earn buy-in. Quotes alone don't earn rigor. The case lands when both arrive together: 135 ratings backed by the words behind them.

02

Build the graph, not the deck.

A heatmap, a dot grid, a tiered scope. Every visualization was a tool for alignment. Something Sales, Marketing, Engineering, and customers could read in 30 seconds and agree on. Graphs traveled where slides couldn't.

03

Interim moves clarify the vision.

Placing a smaller bet today is how I learned what to bet bigger on later. Ship something credible now. The north star stays sharper because of it, not in spite of it.

04

AI prototyping changed the math.

A working base prototype in two days. Two parallel directions in five. The cost of testing a "maybe" dropped to almost zero, so we tested both. Research picked the survivor.

05

Risk has a denominator.

135 ratings turned "this feels risky" into "this is 1-in-135." That single Scared rating on the Changelog was the signal worth slowing down for. Everything else, we shipped with confidence.

06

Alignment is the deliverable.

The research report became the asset Sales referenced, Marketing quoted, and Engineering scoped against. Customers saw their words land in the bet. The alignment was the work.

Thanks for reading :)
